LAW OFFICES OF

NIELSEN, MERKSAMER,
HODGSON, PARRINELLO & MUELLER
A PARTNERSHIP INCLUDING A PROFESSIONAL CORPORATION

MARIN COUNTY
591 REDWOOD HIGHWAY, #4000
MILL VALLEY, CALIFORNIA 94941
TELEPHONE (415) 389-6800

770 L STREET, SUITE 800
SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814
TELEPHONE (916) 466-6752

SAN FRANCISCO
650 CALIFORNIA STREET, SUITE 2650
SAN FRANCISCO, CALIFORNIA 94108
TELEPHONE (415) 989-6800

February 21, 1991

John Wallace, Esq.
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804-0807

Re: Request For Confirmation of Advice

Dear Mr. Wallace:

     This letter is to confirm advice rendered during our
telephone discussion on February 12, 1991, about the new
legislation limiting gifts to public officials. (Government Code
§§ 89500 et. seq.)¹ Our discussion centered on the exemptions
from limits contained in section 89506.

     In summary, you advised that the new law limiting
gifts, and the exceptions thereto, was not intended by the
legislature to abrogate current exceptions from disclosure and
disqualification. Specifically, if a client of this firm invited
a state elected official to make a speech at a location within
California, and provided only that travel, meals and
accomodations necessary for, and in connection with, the speech,
the exceptions from reporting contained in regulations 18623 and
18728 would continue to apply. Further, pursuant to regulation
18728, the value of these items would not count toward the
threshold for disqualification in section 87103, nor would the
value be included in calculating compliance with the new
limitations in Government Code sections 89503 and 89504.

     In short, you advised that section 89506(c) does not
impose new disclosure requirements where none existed before or
redefine as gifts (or activity expenses) expenditures exempted
under existing regulations 18728 and 18623. Rather, section
89506(c) provides an exception from limitations for additional
expenditures (such as reimbursement for interstate travel) not
covered by regulations 18623 and 18728, provided that these

_________________________
¹ All further statutory references are to the Government
Code unless otherwise indicated.

John Wallace, Esq.
February 22, 1991
Page two


additional expenditures not covered by the existing regulations
are disclosed.²

     We would appreciate confirmation of this advice at your
earliest convenience.  Please call if you need clarification.

                                        Very truly yours,

                                        [signature]
                                        JUDITH SPROUL DAVIS

JSD:kab
5909.01




_____________________
     ² It is our understanding that the Commission staff is
developing a special "travel schedule" for reporting expenditures
referred to in section 89506, but has not yet done so.

FPPC

FEB 06 1991

LAW OFFICES OF

NIELSEN, MERKSAMER,
HODGSON, PARRINELLO & MUELLER

770 L STREET, SUITE 800

A PARTNERSHIP INCLUDING A PROFESSIONAL CORPORATION

SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814

MARIN COUNTY

650 CALIFORNIA STREET, SUITE 2650

SAN FRANCISCO

591 REDWOOD HIGHWAY, #4000

TELEPHONE (916) 446-6752

SAN FRANCISCO, CALIFORNIA 94108

MILL VALLEY, CALIFORNIA 94941

TELEPHONE (415) 389-6800

TELEPHONE (415) 989-6800

March 5, 1991

Mr. John Wallace
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, CA 95814

91-143

Re:  Regulations Regarding Gifts

Dear John:

     This letter seeks confirmation of telephone advice
provided earlier this month and discussed in particular with you
on February 27 and March 1, 1991.  You advised generally that
current regulations applicable to the reporting and definition of
gifts will apply in calculating the value of gifts to determine
compliance with the limits on gifts in Government Code sections
89500 et. seq.  (You noted that the staff would at some point be
bringing this general advice to the full Commission.)

     Specifically, you advised that a gift to a spouse or
family member of a public official is not a gift to the official
for purposes of determining compliance with the limitations in
sections 89501, 89504 and 89505 if the procedures prescribed in 2
Cal. Code Regs. § 18726.2 are followed.  That is, a gift of a
ticket to a special event which is delivered by mail or
accompanied by a written communication to a member of an
official's family is not a gift to the official if the family
member's name (or familial designation - i.e. "spouse") appears
in the address on the envelope or in the communication tendering
the gift; the gift is intended to be used by the family member
and the public official does not exercise discretion or control
over the disposition of the gift by giving the ticket to someone
other than the donor-designated member of his or her family.

     On a related matter, we discussed on February 28, 1991
and again on March 1, 1991, the issue of the gift of a ticket for
a specific event to an individual recommended to the donor by a
public official.  For example, a public official invited to
attend an event might request a ticket for a friend.  You advised
that, in considering and granting this request, if the donor
followed the same procedures as prescribed for gifts to family
members, the gift to the friend would not be deemed a gift to the
official for purposes of calculating the gift limit.  If the
public official identifies the individual; the donor
independently decides whether or not to give the ticket as

Mr. John Wallace
February 28, 1991
Page 2


requested; and the ticket is mailed directly to that individual
(not to the public official) or is otherwise tendered to that
individual with a communication making clear that the ticket is
intended only for his or her use and is not transferable to the
public official for other disposition, then the gift of the
ticket is to that individual -- not to the public official.¹ You
discussed this advice with other members of the FPPC staff and
confirmed it on March 1, 1991 by telephone.

We would appreciate written confirmation of your advice
on gifts to family members and friends of public officials at
your earliest convenience.

Thank you for your courtesy.

Very truly yours,

[signature]
JUDITH SPROUL DAVIS

JSD:kah
6278.03
5909.01


_______________________
¹You analogized this to the situation where one candidate
requests a contributor (over whom he or she has no direction and
control) to make a contribution to another candidate: the
contribution is to the candidate to whom the contribution is in
fact made -- not to the candidate recommending the contribution.

California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

March 12, 1990


Judith Spreul-Davis
Nielsen, Merksamer, Hodgson, et al.
770 L Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, CA 95814

Re: Letter No. 91-137

Dear Ms. Spreul-Davis:

Your letter requesting advice under the Political Reform Act
was received on March 6, 1990 by the Fair Political Practices
Commission. If you have any questions about your advice request,
you may contact John W. Illace, an attorney in the Legal Division,
directly at (916) 322-5911.

We try to answer all advice requests promptly. Therefore,
unless your request poses particularly complex legal questions, or
more information is needed, you should expect a response within 21
working days if your request seeks formal written advice. If more
information is needed, the person assigned to prepare a response
for your request will contact you shortly to advise you as to
information needed. If your request is for informal assistance,
we will answer it as quickly as we can. (See Commission
Regulation 18329 (2 Cal. Code of Regs. Sec. 18329).)

You also should be aware that your letter and our response
are public records which may be disclosed to the public upon
receipt of a proper request for disclosure.

Very truly yours,

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel
SH:jl

425 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660
